sections.title: Use
items: singular countable nouns that are not specific or not yet known to the listener: I'm looking for **a** good restaurant around here.

sections.title: Use
items: used instead of *a* when the following word begins with a vowel sound: She waited for **an** entire hour.

sections.title: Use
items: singular countable nouns (specific): Have you been to **the** new café on the high street?
plural countable nouns (specific): Did you read **the** messages I sent you?
uncountable nouns (specific): She handed **the** money to the cashier and walked out.

sections.title: Use
items: plural countable nouns (general): Smartphones have changed the way we communicate.
uncountable nouns (general): Regular exercise is essential for good health.

sections.title: Places
rows: seas (the Atlantic), rivers (the Amazon), areas (the Antarctic), some countries (the USA, the UK), public buildings (the theatre), the Earth, the world, the sky, the moon, the sun, the sea, the environment
towns and cities (Moscow), most countries (France), continents (Europe), streets (Baker Street), planets (Mars)

sections.title: Activities
rows: have a job, work as a ...
on the radio, the media, play the piano
go to work, on TV, go shopping, play tennis, listen to music, go to school, be at school, be at university, school subjects (maths)

sections.title: Time
rows: in the morning/afternoon/evening, on the 20th March, in the 1950s

sections.title: People
rows: the King, the Prime Minister, the army, the navy, the police, the Germans, the English
become king, he's English, speak English

exercises.title: A: a, an or the
exercises.instructions: Complete each sentence with a, an or the.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each sentence with a, an or the.

items.sentence: We stayed in ...... amazing hotel near the beach.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is first mention + vowel sound, so the correct answer is "an".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Could you open ...... door for me, please?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is specific door we can see, so the correct answer is "the".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "the" is the only word or phrase that fits both grammar and meaning in: "Could you open ...... door for me, please?".

items.sentence: My uncle is ...... architect in Barcelona.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is job + vowel sound, so the correct answer is "an".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.

explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "an" is the only word or phrase that fits both grammar and meaning in: "My uncle is ...... architect in Barcelona.".

items.sentence: Where are ...... tickets for the concert?

explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence: She waited for ...... hour outside the cinema.

explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "an" is the only word or phrase that fits both grammar and meaning in: "She waited for ...... hour outside the cinema.".

exercises.title: B: Article or no article

items.sentence: ...... sandwiches on the table are for the guests.

explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

exercises.title: C: Circle the correct word
exercises.instructions: Choose the correct option in each sentence.
exercises.studentInstruction: Choose the correct option in each sentence.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The next word begins with a vowel sound, so we need the indefinite article an.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Use a before consonant sounds and an before vowel sounds — listen to the sound, not just the letter.

wrongOptions.a: A is used before consonant sounds, not before a vowel sound here.
wrongOptions.an: An is only used before vowel sounds.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Say the next word aloud — if it starts with a vowel sound, choose an.

optionContrast.a: "a" doesn't fit here → "an" does, because A is used before consonant sounds, not before a vowel sound here.

items.originalSentence: We waited for **a / an** hour in the rain.
items.completedSentence: We waited for an hour in the rain.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The next word begins with a vowel sound, so we need the indefinite article an.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Use a before consonant sounds and an before vowel sounds — listen to the sound, not just the letter.

wrongOptions.a: A is used before consonant sounds, not before a vowel sound here.
wrongOptions.an: An is only used before vowel sounds.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Say the next word aloud — if it starts with a vowel sound, choose an.

optionContrast.a: "a" doesn't fit here → "an" does, because A is used before consonant sounds, not before a vowel sound here.

items.originalSentence: He needs **a / an** uniform for his new job.
items.completedSentence: He needs a uniform for his new job.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The next word begins with a vowel sound, so we need the indefinite article an.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Use a before consonant sounds and an before vowel sounds — listen to the sound, not just the letter.

wrongOptions.a: A is used before consonant sounds, not before a vowel sound here.
wrongOptions.an: An is only used before vowel sounds.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Say the next word aloud — if it starts with a vowel sound, choose an.

optionContrast.an: "an" doesn't fit here → "a" does, because An is only used before vowel sounds.

items.originalSentence: She paid with **a / an** euro note.
items.completedSentence: She paid with a euro note.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The next word begins with a vowel sound, so we need the indefinite article an.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Use a before consonant sounds and an before vowel sounds — listen to the sound, not just the letter.

wrongOptions.a: A is used before consonant sounds, not before a vowel sound here.
wrongOptions.an: An is only used before vowel sounds.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Say the next word aloud — if it starts with a vowel sound, choose an.

optionContrast.an: "an" doesn't fit here → "a" does, because An is only used before vowel sounds.

items.completedSentence: Let's go to the theatre tonight.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: We are talking about one specific thing both speakers know about, so the definite article is required.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Use the when the listener knows which person, place, or thing you mean.

wrongOptions.-: No article is wrong here — English needs the before a specific known thing.
wrongOptions.the: The is wrong only when no specific thing is meant — that is not the case here.

optionContrast.-: "-" doesn't fit here → "the" does, because No article is wrong here — English needs the before a specific known thing.

items.originalSentence: I saw the interview on **the / -** TV.
items.completedSentence: I saw the interview on - TV.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: We are talking about one specific thing both speakers know about, so the definite article is required.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Use the when the listener knows which person, place, or thing you mean.

wrongOptions.-: No article is wrong here — English needs the before a specific known thing.
wrongOptions.the: The is wrong only when no specific thing is meant — that is not the case here.

optionContrast.the: "the" doesn't fit here → "-" does, because The is wrong only when no specific thing is meant — that is not the case here.

items.originalSentence: My dad is at **the / -** work right now.
items.completedSentence: My dad is at - work right now.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: We are talking about one specific thing both speakers know about, so the definite article is required.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Use the when the listener knows which person, place, or thing you mean.

wrongOptions.-: No article is wrong here — English needs the before a specific known thing.
wrongOptions.the: The is wrong only when no specific thing is meant — that is not the case here.

optionContrast.the: "the" doesn't fit here → "-" does, because The is wrong only when no specific thing is meant — that is not the case here.

items.originalSentence: We heard the news on **the / -** radio.
items.completedSentence: We heard the news on the radio.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: We are talking about one specific thing both speakers know about, so the definite article is required.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Use the when the listener knows which person, place, or thing you mean.

wrongOptions.-: No article is wrong here — English needs the before a specific known thing.
wrongOptions.the: The is wrong only when no specific thing is meant — that is not the case here.

optionContrast.-: "-" doesn't fit here → "the" does, because No article is wrong here — English needs the before a specific known thing.

explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past simple is used for finished actions at a specific past time.

exercises.title: F: Error correction
exercises.instructions: Each sentence has one article mistake. Write the correct article, or '–' if no article is needed.
exercises.studentInstruction: Each sentence has one article mistake. Write the correct article, or '–' if no article is needed.

items.sentence: He is an European football player.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The error is in "an" — european starts with /j/ sound, so the correction is "a".
explanationContent.grammarFocus: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.

wrongOptions.an: "an" is the highlighted error — use "a" here instead.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The error is in "an", not elsewhere — replace it with "a" and read the full sentence to check it sounds natural.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.

items.sentence: The French is spoken in several countries.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The error is in "The" — language name, so the correction is "–".
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

wrongOptions.The: "The" is the highlighted error — use "–" here instead.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The error is in "The", not elsewhere — replace it with "–" and read the full sentence to check it sounds natural.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.

items.sentence: She works as the receptionist in a hotel.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The error is in "the" — job in general, so the correction is "a".
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

wrongOptions.the: "the" does not fit this slot — the correct word is "a".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not leave "the" — the rule here needs "a" in the highlighted slot.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.

items.sentence: We could see a sun from the mountain.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The error is in "a" — unique object, so the correction is "the".
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

wrongOptions.a: "a" does not fit this slot — the correct word is "the".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not leave "a" — the rule here needs "the" in the highlighted slot.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.

items.sentence: They visited a British Museum in London.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The error is in "a" — famous building, so the correction is "the".
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

wrongOptions.a: "a" is the highlighted error — use "the" here instead.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The error is in "a", not elsewhere — replace it with "the" and read the full sentence to check it sounds natural.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.

items.sentence: My grandmother listens to a radio every evening.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The error is in "a" — media, so the correction is "the".
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

wrongOptions.a: "a" does not fit this slot — the correct word is "the".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not leave "a" — the rule here needs "the" in the highlighted slot.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Word-pattern errors are fixed chunks — memorise the preposition that follows the key adjective or verb.

items.sentence: The students are going to the school now.

explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

wrongOptions.the: "the" is the highlighted error — use "–" here instead.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The error is in "the", not elsewhere — replace it with "–" and read the full sentence to check it sounds natural.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.

wrongOptions.the: "the" is the highlighted error — use "–" here instead.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The error is in "the", not elsewhere — replace it with "–" and read the full sentence to check it sounds natural.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.

exercises.title: G: Error correction
exercises.instructions: Each sentence has one article mistake. Find the error and write the whole sentence correctly.
exercises.studentInstruction: Each sentence has one article mistake. Find the error and write the whole sentence correctly.

items.sentence: He bought an new laptop yesterday.
items.answer: He bought a new laptop yesterday.
acceptedAnswers: He bought a new laptop yesterday.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The error is in "an" — consonant sound, so the correction is "He bought a new laptop yesterday.".
explanationContent.grammarFocus: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.

wrongOptions.an: "an" does not fit this slot — the correct word is "He bought a new laptop yesterday.".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not leave "an" — the rule here needs "He bought a new laptop yesterday." in the highlighted slot.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.

items.sentence: We had dinner at a hotel restaurant.
items.answer: We had dinner at the hotel restaurant.
acceptedAnswers: We had dinner at the hotel restaurant.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The error is in "a" — specific restaurant in the hotel, so the correction is "We had dinner at the hotel restaurant.".
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

wrongOptions.a: "a" is the highlighted error — use "We had dinner at the hotel restaurant." here instead.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The error is in "a", not elsewhere — replace it with "We had dinner at the hotel restaurant." and read the full sentence to check it sounds natural.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.

items.sentence: My uncle lives in the Italy.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The error is in "the" — country name, so the correction is "My uncle lives in Italy.".
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

wrongOptions.the: "the" is the highlighted error — use "My uncle lives in Italy." here instead.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The error is in "the", not elsewhere — replace it with "My uncle lives in Italy." and read the full sentence to check it sounds natural.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.

items.sentence: Can your sister play a piano?

explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

wrongOptions.a: "a" is the highlighted error — use "Can your sister play the piano?" here instead.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The error is in "a", not elsewhere — replace it with "Can your sister play the piano?" and read the full sentence to check it sounds natural.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.

items.sentence: She waited for a hour before calling again.
items.answer: She waited for an hour before calling again.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: An hour (silent h).
explanationContent.grammarFocus: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.

wrongOptions.a: "a" is the highlighted error — use "She waited for an hour before calling again." here instead.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The error is in "a", not elsewhere — replace it with "She waited for an hour before calling again." and read the full sentence to check it sounds natural.

items.sentence: A students in this class are very friendly.
items.answer: The students in this class are very friendly.
acceptedAnswers: The students in this class are very friendly.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The error is in "A" — plural noun, so the correction is "The students in this class are very friendly.".
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Check tense and subject–verb agreement — the highlighted form must match the time clues in the sentence.

wrongOptions.A: "A" does not fit this slot — the correct word is "The students in this class are very friendly.".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not leave "A" — the rule here needs "The students in this class are very friendly." in the highlighted slot.

exercises.title: H: Key word transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence using the keyword given.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence using the keyword given.

items.sentence:
My mother works as doctor in a hospital. **A**
My mother works as ...... in a hospital.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is job, so the correct answer is "a doctor".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "a doctor", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
We heard the weather forecast on radio. **THE**
We heard the weather forecast on ...... .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is media, so the correct answer is "the radio".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "the radio", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
Her grandfather is in the prison now. **NO**
Her grandfather is in ...... now.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is in prison (institution), so the correct answer is "prison".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
I enjoy reading books about history. **NO**
I enjoy reading books about ...... .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is subject in general, so the correct answer is "history".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "the USA", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

exercises.title: I: Mixed article practice

exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each sentence with a, an, the or – (no article).

items.sentence: He plays ...... violin in the school orchestra.

explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: I ate ...... orange after lunch.

items.sentence: My sister works as ...... accountant.

items.sentence: We visited ...... Colosseum during our holiday.

items.sentence: ...... Pacific Ocean is enormous.

items.sentence: They go to ...... school on foot.

explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.

items.sentence: He studied at ...... Cambridge University.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is university name, so the correct answer is "-".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "-" is the only word or phrase that fits both grammar and meaning in: "He studied at ...... Cambridge University.".

items.sentence: It was ...... best concert I've ever seen.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is superlative, so the correct answer is "the".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.
